texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions are estimated to increase by approximately 30% by 2040. Based on this increase, the capture and storage of CO2 are presented as a suitable opportunity to decrease emissions. This study o ff ers a preliminary overview of state-of-the-art CO2 capture patents during the past decade (2007 -2017). More than 28,000 patents and 5000 scienti fi c documents were collected, analysed and classi fi ed in terms of the Cooperative Patent Classi fi cation (CPC) criteria. Most patent generation activity is concentrated in capture technologies that use absorption and adsorption chemical processes (within 35% and 30% of the collected documents, respectively). Moreover, companies such as Mitsubishi Hitachi Power Systems Europe GmbH, Alstom Technology Ltd., ExxonMobil Research and Engineering Company and Air Liquide SA dominate the development of these main capture technologies. Specialized companies such as CO2 Solutions Inc., Akermin Inc. and UOP LLC are leaders in membrane systems or biological processes; however, there are fewer publications related to these technologies. Most of the patent activity is occurring in the USA, Japan, China and Korea (responsible for 64% of the innovations); also highlighting the role of international o ffi ces such as the World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO) and the European Patent O ffi ce (EPO) (with 22% of patent generation). The growing knowledge of patent generation over the past decade indicates that CO2 capture technologies have inspired environmental and economic interest, making it possible to develop established processes that can be implemented by the current industry and to optimize the existing industries. The idea of knowing what are the most active companies in terms of patent production and the fi elds where they are working, give competitors information about the identi fi cation of development trends and economically viable investigation lines.

texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The anthropogenic accumulation of greenhouse gas emissions has grown exponentially since the preindustrial period. The utilization of fossil fuels and industrial processes have risen approximately 2% annually over previous decades. More recently, world energy use related to CO2 emissions has increased at an average annual rate of 1.0% from 2012 to 2040 according to the IEA-2016 Reference case (Fig. 1) [1]. On average, OECD emissions increased by 0.3% per year, and non-OECD emissions increased by 1.5% per year. The situation of carbonization, known as the 450 Scenario, is detailed in WEO-2016 and sets out the objective of limiting the increase in the average global temperature to 2 °C above pre-industrial levels by 2100 [1].
texts.text: The anthropogenic accumulation of greenhouse gas emissions has grown exponentially since the preindustrial period. The utilization of fossil fuels and industrial processes have risen approximately 2% annually over previous decades. More recently, world energy use related to CO2 emissions has increased at an average annual rate of 1.0% from 2012 to 2040 according to the IEA-2016 Reference case (Fig. 1) [1]. On average, OECD emissions increased by 0.3% per year, and non-OECD emissions increased by 1.5% per year. The situation of carbonization, known as the 450 Scenario, is detailed in WEO-2016 and sets out the objective of limiting the increase in the average global temperature to 2 °C above pre-industrial levels by 2100 [1].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One of the challenges of the 21st century is to meet the energy needs of a continuously growing world population while simultaneously decreasing CO2 emissions in accordance with agreements reached at successive climate summits.
texts.text: One of the challenges of the 21st century is to meet the energy needs of a continuously growing world population while simultaneously decreasing CO2 emissions in accordance with agreements reached at successive climate summits.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One reduction strategy relates to CO2 capture and storage. Yang et al. [2] explained three options to reduce total CO2 emissions into the atmosphere, i.e. by energy intensity reduction, carbon intensity reduction and CO2 sequestration enhancement. The fi rst option requires an e ffi cient use of energy, as explained in several researches [3,4]; whereas the second option requires switching to the use of non-fossil fuels such as hydrogen and renewable energy, studied by several authors [5 -9]. The third option involves the development of technologies to capture more CO2.
texts.text: One reduction strategy relates to CO2 capture and storage. Yang et al. [2] explained three options to reduce total CO2 emissions into the atmosphere, i.e. by energy intensity reduction, carbon intensity reduction and CO2 sequestration enhancement. The fi rst option requires an e ffi cient use of energy, as explained in several researches [3,4]; whereas the second option requires switching to the use of non-fossil fuels such as hydrogen and renewable energy, studied by several authors [5 -9]. The third option involves the development of technologies to capture more CO2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To perform sequestration e ff ectively, large volumes of gas are required. For all industrial applications where CO2 separation is an intrinsic part of production, CO2 capture processes are mature and commercially available. In other industrial applications, CO2 separation processes are less advanced or would require a considerable redesign of traditional processes (Table 1) [10]. In addition, there are several concrete applications where the need of increasing the capacities of their facilities or pilot plants are evidencing, mainly in biomass and biofuel industry (Enerkem Inc., Steeper Energy Aps. or Susteen Technologies GmbH). The main issue for technologies that do not separate CO2 is the economic impact that occurs when incorporating this new technology to the existing industrial processes or energy generators. According to studies examining this issue, CO2 capture costs represent the main investment (see Table 2).

texts.orig: Due to the high cost of conventional capture processes, numerous research articles and reports have been published on the sequestration and separation of CO2. The separation technologies include adsorption, absorption, cryogenic separation, membranes and, more recently, biological capture.

texts.orig: Related to the technology of adsorption, the use of zeolites was also studied in some papers [34 -39]. Siriwardane et al. [40] worked on capture for adsorption at moderate temperatures, whilst Yong et al. [41] carried out a study related to CO2 adsorption at high temperatures. Duan et al. and Jiang et al. [42,43] investigated the use of lithium silicates and amino acids for CO2 adsorption processes, respectively. Studies on chemical-looping combustion (CLC) were also found [45 -52], underlining the outstanding review of the di ff erent CLC processes and prototypes up to 2010, carried out by Adanez et al.[44]. The Calcium-Looping (CaL) technology was deeply studied by Blamey et al. and Perejón et al. [52,53]. Oxy-fuel combustion technology was highlighted in multiple studies [55 -60], outlining the work of Yin and Yan [54],where the fundamental issues of this CCS technology are appropriately explained.

texts.orig: Other new possibility is the biological capture, which was reviewed by Goli et al. [61]. Many of the works related to this fi eld studied the use of microalgae as enzyme promoters. Most of the microalgae and cyanobacteria contained the enzyme carbonic anhydrase (CA), which is the most e ffi cient catalyst for CO2 hydration and dehydration (absorption and desorption) [62]. Cowan et al. and Robert et al. [63,64] examined options to increase the e ffi ciency of other industrial processes. Bong et al. [65] developed a system where the enzyme CA works as a catalyst to accelerate the rate of CO2 hydration for subsequent fi xation into stable mineral carbonates. Several studies [61,66 -68] also demonstrated that the con fi guration of bioreactors is one of the main factors controlling the biomass productivity of photosynthetic cultures grown under di ff erent conditions.

texts.orig: The main purpose of this paper is to study the evolution of numerous patents and assignees as well as to analyse the patent database, in function of countries/o ffi ces and assignee nationality, focusing on a new perspective never taken into account before: the point of view of companies and their patent production. In addition, this work explores the top ten patent list, according to the Cooperative Patent Classi fi cation (CPC), in terms of the number of citations and their knowledge contributions.

texts.orig: To perform this study, more than one hundred international databases were consulted, in contrast to certain studies such as [94,96], to obtain a more complete overview of patents. The international databases include the o ffi ces of the USA (USPTO), Europe (EPO), and worldwide (WIPO) and the national o ffi ces of France, Germany, United Kingdom, China, Korea, Japan and Spain. The used databases were: (i) Derwent World Patents Index ® (DWPI SM ), created by Thomson Reuters, which includes more than 28 million patent families and covers over 50 patent o ffi ces and 2 scienti fi c literature sources; (ii) PatBase (Minesoft and RNR Group), one of the most used database covering about 56 million patent families, including 100 million complete patent documents of more than 100 o ffi ces; (iii) PatentScope, which allows access to information of international patent applications through the Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT); (iv) ESPACENET, which o ff ers more than 80 million patent documents since 1836; (v) Web of Science, created by Thomson Reuters, which facilitates access to di ff erent databases with information about citations of scienti fi c publications.

texts.orig: It is noticed that each national and international o ffi ce classi fi es their patents according objective criteria. However, as this classi fi cation is carried out by di ff erent examiners, from di ff erent technical sectors, it provides a certain margin to the subjective interpretation of some concepts. In addition, during the investigations, only those fi les (patents) that have been already published can be detected. The period between the submission of a patent application and its publication varies widely among the di ff erent o ffi ces, although in most cases, publication occurs at least 18 months after the application date. These two facts lead to the introduction of some margin of error to any search process; therefore, results have been carefully interpreted.

texts.orig: In general, patent applicants intend to obtain a return on their investment by either the sale or assignment of their patent rights. However, there appears to be more certainty that a patent will be put into practice if it is applied for by a company rather than by an individual. For this reason, it is useful to carry out a classi fi cation of the patents by applicant type. This classi fi cation allows a preliminary idea of the connection degree between the invention and the real economy to be obtained, and it also provides the magnitude of business world awareness of the patent and promotion of CO2 capture technology.

texts.orig: Patents have been classi fi ed in function of the following applicant types: (1) inventor, (2) companies and (3) universities or research centres. Fig. 3 shows the portion of patents based on the type of applicant. The main type of applicant is the individual inventor for biological capture, membrane or recti fi cation areas, representing approximately 60%. Companies represent the main applicant type (50%) for chemical capture, absorption and adsorption groups. This high percentage of contributions indicates strong implantation and market entry; thus, some technologies can be considered mature since companies likely have had previous patents related to each of the fi elds. A decrease in the percentage of individual patents in favour of company patents is an indicator of the growing complexity and inversion of this technology. More speci fi cally, a single individual cannot develop a technology alone, and thus, there is a growing consideration of this

texts.orig: Regarding the corporations involved in CO2 capture development, Tables 4 -6 show the reference groups for the abovementioned technologies. As a general point of view, the 20 companies included in Tables 5 and 6 (Appendix) lead the generation of patents, with approximately two-thirds of the total activity. Firms such as Mitsubishi Hitachi Power Systems Europe GmbH and Alstom Technology Ltd. or ExxonMobil Research and Engineering Company and Air Liquide SA dominate in terms of the development of absorption and adsorption technologies, respectively. Sectors where activity is low (biological processes or membrane capture) are led by specialized groups such as CO2 Solutions Inc., Novozymes A/S and UOP LLC.

texts.orig: Focusing on the companies that dominate the development of biological separation (Y02C 10/02), the fi rms that produce equipment that stand out are Alstom Technology Ltd. and CO2 Solutions Inc., with 9% and 8% of the published patents, respectively. These companies are followed by Novozymes A/S, Akermin Inc., Morphic Technologies AG and Bio Fuel Systems SL, which together make up to 15% of the sector ' s activity. In this subgroup, the most frequently cited patent is US20080009055A1 [110] of Greenfuel Technologies Co., which proposes integrated photobioreactor-based pollution mitigation and oil extraction processes and systems. This patent protects a photobioreactor system operating with a photosynthetic organism (i.e., algae) to the capture of pollutant compounds such as CO2 or NOx. It is noted that the term ' phototrophic organism ' or ' biomass, ' as used therein, includes any organism capable of photosynthetic growth in a liquid phase (plant cells and uni- or multi-cellular micro-organisms, including algae, cyanobacteria, Lemna and Euglena ).

texts.orig: For chemical separation technology (Y02C 10/04), the main corporations are related to power generation and oil companies, with Mitsubishi Hitachi Power Systems Europe GmbH and Alstom Technology Ltd. representing almost 14% of the patents for chemical capture and separation of CO2. These companies are followed by General Electric Co. and ExxonMobil Research and Engineering Company as well as Kansai Electric Power Co. Inc. and Siemens AG, which together make up 13% of the collected information. The most cited patents of this technology are US20080031801A1 [111] and US20080138265A1 [112], from the University of Columbia. Both patents provide new methods or processes for extracting, reducing, capturing, sequestering or disposing of carbon dioxide (CO2), particularly from the ambient air, gas that is emitted from a smoke stack or an exhaust pipe. The fi rst patent involves the use of chemical processes, mineral sequestration, and solid and liquid sorbents also described for extracting and/or capturing CO2 via condensation on solid surfaces at low temperatures. The second patent describes a method for extracting and/or capturing CO2 through a basic solution that absorbs carbon dioxide, producing a carbonate solution. The product is causticized, increasing the temperature to release CO2, and the base is regenerated by the hydration of solid components.

texts.orig: On the other hand, there are a small group of companies with most of their activity focused on the technology of CO2 capture by membranes (Y02C 10/10). These companies account for approximately 60% of the publications and 51% of the patent families, and they include UOP LLC (a subsidiary of Honeywell International INC), General Electric Co., Kilimanjaro Energy Inc. and FujiFilm Corporation, which has recently experienced growth related to this activity. UOP LLC is the main applicant within the sector, with 80 patents in the USA. This company owns the Selexol ™ Process patent, which allows the separation of acid gases such as SO2 or CO2 by means of a solvent at a relatively high pressure. Varying the pressure, these gases are released into a controlled atmosphere, which allows them to be reutilized in other processes. In addition to this solvent, the corporation owns other production processes with di ff erent technologies, such as adsorption (UOP MOLSIV APG III adsorbent) or membranes (UOP Separex ™ Membrane System process). One of the most cited patents is the US20080127632A1 ' Carbon Dioxide Capture Systems And Methods ' [116], published in 2008 by General Electric Co. This innovation protects a system for CO2 capture based on a permeoselective membrane, which allows the CO2 to separate from the gas fl ow. Moreover, aiming for process optimization, the expansion of the free CO2 stream is performed in a turbine, which allows the generation of energy. Another signi fi cant invention is US20070004023A1 [117], by Michael C. Trachtenberg, which is related to methods, apparatuses, and reactors to extract and purify gases from mixed gas streams or to feed streams by way of a phase-conversion membrane that selectively absorbs a desired component gas from a mixed stream and converts it into a second phase, thereby isolating and purifying the desired component. The term ' phase conversion ' as used herein includes the conversion of the gas phase to a dissolved gas phase (for example, a gas dissolved in a polymer or dissolved in an aqueous, organic or ionized liquid) or the conversion of gas to an ionized compound or salt dissolved in a liquid.

texts.orig: Finally, in terms of recti fi cation and condensation (Y02C 10/12), the primary company is Air Liquide SA, which is involved in gas production and represents 23% of the patent families and one-quarter of the publications in this fi eld. Other companies in the oil sector, such as ExxonMobil Research and Engineering Company, Shell International Research Maatschappij BV and Alstom Technology Ltd., account for almost 30% of the activity. The sixth position is occupied by Praxair Technology Inc., the fi rst industrial gas supplier in America, and Linde AG. For the selective recuperation of CO2, the RECTISOL ® process was independently developed, but its commercialization (patent and fi rm) is owned by Linde AG and Air Liquide SA. The most cited patent in this fi eld is US20090117024A1 ' Process For The Production Of Hydrogen With Co-Production And Capture Of Carbon Dioxide ' [118], which was published in 2009 by the individual inventors Bjarne Torgersen and Geo ff rey Gerald Weedon. This patent involves a pre-combustion system that produces 99.999% pure hydrogen with CO2 capture of the stream from the hydrocarbons reforming process. Moreover, the patent US20070232706A1 [119] from the company Praxair Technology Inc. provides a method of producing a CO2 product stream from a hydrogen plant. The hydrogen plant incorporates a synthesis gas reactor, a watergas shift reactor located downstream of the synthesis gas reactor and a hydrogen pressure swing adsorption unit to produce a hydrogen product.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The present work on CO2 capture technologies o ff ers a preliminary view of state-of-the-art patents over the last ten years through the analysis of patent documents and use of the Cooperative Patent Classi fi cation (CPC) for their compilation. It is presented as a patent review, where the sort of the provided information is given as a summary of the most useful analysis ' fi ndings from a techno-economical point of view. Patents were collected into more than 7400 patent families (>28,000 documents), and more than 5000 scienti fi c documents from the last decade (January 2007 to March 2017) refer to the processes of separation and capture of CO2,). This information represents approximately 75% of the published state-of-the-art patents, in accordance with the selected search criteria, which were mainly governed by the use of the CPC classi fi cation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The development of an economically viable technology for CO2 capture would be a considerable advance to optimize for other industries since it would contribute to the reduction of greenhouse emissions. Concretely, the emission values for CO2 that have been fi xed for each country could be reached by implementing these technologies in a complementary way in speci fi c existing industries (i.e., plants for energy generation, hydrogen generation, water-to-gas conversion processes, and water treatment). Furthermore, the integration of these technologies would allow the transformation of recent residues into byproducts of added value with the possibility of reutilizing them in other production processes, which would result in more sustainable development of the current industry.
